metadata.title: Association Between Postoperative Complications After Anterior Lumbar Interbody Fusion and Prior Abdominal Surgery: A Retrospective Cohort Study
metadata.abstract: OBJECTIVE: To investigate the association between postoperative complications after ALIF and prior abdominal surgery. SUMMARY OF BACKGROUND DATA: Anterior lumbar interbody fusion (ALIF) is a common spinal surgery associated with a variety of complications. Previous abdominal surgery is thought to influence the rate at which these postoperative complications occur. METHODS: Using PearlDiver, patients were identified who had undergone an ALIF from 2010 to 2024. Patients were separated based on surgical history (cesarean section, non-obstetric abdominal surgery, any abdominal surgery or no prior abdominal surgery). Complications were measured (30 d, 90 d, 1 y). Univariate and multivariate logistic regression were performed. Significance was set at P<0.05. RESULTS: Among 1,123,841 ALIF patients, complications occurred in 2.6% at 30 days, 3.5% at 90 days, and 5.4% at 1 year. On unadjusted analysis, prior non-obstetric abdominal surgery (ABD) or any abdominal surgery (ANYABD) more than doubled complication risk (1-year OR 2.3, 95% CI 2.2-2.4; P<0.0001), while cesarean section (CSEC) showed no significant increase (OR 1.16, 95% CI 0.99-1.35; P=0.07). After adjusting for age, sex, and comorbidities, no significant differences remained. Comorbidity burden (ECI) emerged as the strongest predictor (OR 1.08, 95% CI 1.07-1.09; P<0.0001). CONCLUSIONS: While complications after ALIF are not uncommon, prior studies have suggested a higher risk among patients with a history of abdominal surgery. While raw complication rates appear significantly higher in patients with prior abdominal surgery, these differences become nonsignificant once comorbidity burden is adjusted for. Our findings demonstrate that prior abdominal surgery itself does not independently increase postoperative complications, rather, patients with prior abdominal operations tend to have a greater comorbidity burden, which likely explains their higher unadjusted complication rates.

spine_metadata.main_conclusion: Prior abdominal surgery does not independently increase postoperative complications after ALIF; higher unadjusted complication rates in patients with prior abdominal surgery are attributable to greater comorbidity burden rather than surgical history itself.

chunks.content: OBJECTIVE: To investigate the association between postoperative complications after ALIF and prior abdominal surgery. SUMMARY OF BACKGROUND DATA: Anterior lumbar interbody fusion (ALIF) is a common spinal surgery associated with a variety of complications. Previous abdominal surgery is thought to influence the rate at which these postoperative complications occur.
chunks.summary: Study objective is to examine whether prior abdominal surgery increases postoperative complication risk following ALIF.

chunks.content: METHODS: Using PearlDiver, patients were identified who had undergone an ALIF from 2010 to 2024. Patients were separated based on surgical history (cesarean section, non-obstetric abdominal surgery, any abdominal surgery or no prior abdominal surgery). Complications were measured (30 d, 90 d, 1 y). Univariate and multivariate logistic regression were performed. Significance was set at P<0.05.
chunks.summary: Retrospective cohort study using PearlDiver database with stratification by surgical history and complication assessment at multiple timepoints.

chunks.content: Study population comprised 1,123,841 patients who underwent ALIF between 2010 and 2024. Patients were stratified into four groups: those with no prior abdominal surgery (reference group), those with prior cesarean section, those with prior non-obstetric abdominal surgery, and those with any prior abdominal surgery. The large cohort size enabled sufficient statistical power to detect differences in complication rates across groups.
chunks.summary: Large retrospective cohort of 1.1 million ALIF patients stratified by prior abdominal surgery history.

chunks.content: Overall complication rates across the entire cohort at 30 days, 90 days, and 1 year were 2.6%, 3.5%, and 5.4%, respectively. These rates provide baseline estimates of postoperative morbidity following ALIF in a large contemporary cohort.
chunks.summary: Overall ALIF complication rates are 2.6% at 30 days, 3.5% at 90 days, and 5.4% at 1 year.

chunks.content: On unadjusted analysis, prior non-obstetric abdominal surgery (ABD) or any abdominal surgery (ANYABD) more than doubled 1-year complication risk (OR 2.3, 95% CI 2.2-2.4; P<0.0001). This substantial increase in odds was statistically highly significant and suggests a strong raw association between prior abdominal surgery and postoperative complications after ALIF.
chunks.summary: Unadjusted analysis shows prior non-obstetric or any abdominal surgery more than doubles 1-year complication risk.

chunks.content: Cesarean section (CSEC) showed no significant increase in complication risk compared to no prior surgery (OR 1.16, 95% CI 0.99-1.35; P=0.07). This nonsignificant finding suggests that obstetric abdominal surgery history does not meaningfully increase the risk of postoperative complications following ALIF.
chunks.summary: Prior cesarean section does not significantly increase complication risk after ALIF on unadjusted analysis.

chunks.content: After adjusting for age, sex, and comorbidities in multivariate logistic regression, no significant differences in complication risk remained between groups stratified by prior abdominal surgery history. This critical finding indicates that the apparent association between prior abdominal surgery and complications was confounded by demographic and comorbidity factors.
chunks.summary: Multivariate adjustment for age, sex, and comorbidities eliminates significant differences in complication risk among abdominal surgery groups.

chunks.content: Comorbidity burden, as measured by the Elixhauser Comorbidity Index (ECI), emerged as the strongest independent predictor of postoperative complications following ALIF (OR 1.08, 95% CI 1.07-1.09; P<0.0001). For each additional comorbidity unit, the odds of complications increased by 8%, demonstrating the substantial impact of comorbidity burden on surgical outcomes.
chunks.summary: Comorbidity burden (ECI) is the strongest independent predictor of postoperative complications, with 8% increased odds per unit increase.

chunks.content: The study demonstrates that while prior studies have suggested higher risk in patients with prior abdominal surgery, the raw complication rates do appear significantly elevated in patients with prior abdominal surgery. However, these differences become nonsignificant once comorbidity burden is adjusted for in multivariate analysis, indicating confounding by comorbidity.
chunks.summary: The apparent increased complication risk with prior abdominal surgery is explained by comorbidity burden rather than surgical history.

chunks.content: Our findings demonstrate that prior abdominal surgery itself does not independently increase postoperative complications after ALIF. Rather, patients with prior abdominal operations tend to have a greater comorbidity burden, which likely explains their higher unadjusted complication rates. This distinction is clinically important for patient counseling and risk stratification.
chunks.summary: Prior abdominal surgery is not an independent risk factor for ALIF complications; higher rates reflect comorbidity burden, not surgical history.

chunks.content: The use of PearlDiver database enabled analysis of over 1.1 million ALIF patients across a 14-year period (2010-2024), providing substantial statistical power and contemporary real-world data. This large sample size allows for robust subgroup analyses and adjustment for multiple confounding variables, strengthening the validity of the findings.
chunks.summary: Large database study with over 1 million patients provides substantial statistical power and contemporary clinical data.

chunks.content: CONCLUSIONS: While complications after ALIF are not uncommon, occurring in 2.6% at 30 days, 3.5% at 90 days, and 5.4% at 1 year, prior abdominal surgery history should not be used as an independent risk factor for postoperative complications. Clinicians should focus on comorbidity burden assessment for better risk stratification and patient counseling regarding ALIF outcomes.
chunks.summary: Prior abdominal surgery is not an independent risk factor for ALIF complications; comorbidity burden should guide risk assessment.

chunks.content: Study population: 1,123,841 ALIF patients from 2010-2024. Stratification: Four groups based on abdominal surgical history - no prior surgery (reference), cesarean section only, non-obstetric abdominal surgery, and any prior abdominal surgery. Outcomes assessed: postoperative complications at 30 days, 90 days, and 1 year. Statistical analysis: Univariate and multivariate logistic regression with significance set at P<0.05.
chunks.summary: Study population comprised 1.1 million ALIF patients stratified by prior abdominal surgery history with complications assessed at three timepoints using logistic regression.

chunks.content: Unadjusted complication risks: Prior non-obstetric abdominal surgery or any prior abdominal surgery - OR 2.3 (95% CI: 2.2-2.4, P<0.0001), demonstrating more than doubled risk. Prior cesarean section - OR 1.16 (95% CI: 0.99-1.35, P=0.07), showing no significant increase. Adjusted analysis: After multivariate adjustment for age, sex, and comorbidities, no significant differences remained between surgical history groups (P>0.05). Comorbidity burden (ECI) - strongest predictor: OR 1.08 per unit (95% CI: 1.07-1.09, P<0.0001).
chunks.summary: Unadjusted analysis shows doubled complication risk with prior non-obstetric surgery (OR 2.3), but significance disappears after adjustment; comorbidity burden is strongest independent predictor (OR 1.08).

statistics.is_significant: True for unadjusted non-obstetric surgery; False for adjusted analysis

effect_measure.label: Non-obstetric surgery: OR 2.3 (95% CI: 2.2-2.4); Comorbidity: OR 1.08 (95% CI: 1.07-1.09)

chunks.content: Complication rates by timepoint: 30-day complication rate 2.6% across entire cohort; 90-day complication rate 3.5% across entire cohort; 1-year complication rate 5.4% across entire cohort. These rates represent the cumulative morbidity in a contemporary large cohort of ALIF patients. The progressive increase from 30 to 90 days to 1 year reflects delayed presentations of complications including infections, hardware issues, and other delayed morbidities.
chunks.summary: Complication rates increase over time: 2.6% at 30 days, 3.5% at 90 days, and 5.4% at 1 year, reflecting delayed presentations of various morbidities.

chunks.content: Comparison of unadjusted vs. adjusted odds ratios for prior abdominal surgery: Unadjusted OR for non-obstetric/any abdominal surgery 2.3 (95% CI: 2.2-2.4), appearing to show strong association. However, after multivariate adjustment for age, sex, and comorbidities, the odds ratios for prior abdominal surgery categories became nonsignificant, dropping toward 1.0 and losing statistical significance. This pattern is classic for confounding, where the apparent effect disappears when confounders are controlled. Comorbidity burden maintained strong significance (OR 1.08), indicating it is the true predictor, not surgical history.
chunks.summary: Unadjusted analysis shows strong association between prior abdominal surgery and complications, but this association is entirely explained by comorbidity burden in adjusted analysis.

effect_measure.label: OR 2.3 (95% CI: 2.2-2.4) unadjusted; nonsignificant after adjustment
